0%loading the showpiece
Showpiece0/0
    ← Vibes
    quietloud
    HUGE

    hush

    This is display type -- up to 22vw tall. It's the first thing you noticed, and that's kind of the whole point. Drag the dial up top and watch the same page go from agency-loud to luxury-quiet.

    ❯ Huge display typeEssential

    If you've only got one thing to say, might as well say it big. Oversized type sets the hierarchy before anyone's even read a word.

    How it's done

    A single word at font-size: calc(var(--sp-display-vw) * 1vw), with two faces (Anton + Fraunces) cross-faded by the --dial variable.

    scroll ↓

    The wait you didn't need.

    This page opened with a loading screen counting up to 100%. Here's the confession though -- nothing was actually loading. It was ready before it even started counting; we made up the wait so the reveal would feel earned. It's an old trick from the theatre, just ported over to the web.

    ❯ Preloader⚠ Manipulative

    A loading screen covering real work is a courtesy. One that makes up the wait -- fake percentages, a spinner over a page that already rendered -- is just theatre, billed to the one account a visitor can't top up: their time. Ours is deliberately the fake kind, so you can actually feel the difference.

    How it's done

    A full-screen overlay counts 0→100 with GSAP while the finished page waits behind it, then fades. It's hidden completely when JavaScript is off -- no stuck screen -- and it doesn't run under prefers-reduced-motion.

    Feel that glide?

    The whole page is on rails here -- no abrupt jumps, momentum eases in and out instead. That's Lenis smooth-scrolling, and it's honestly why this already feels "expensive."

    ❯ Smooth scrollSituational

    Makes sense on a showpiece, where the scroll basically IS the experience. On a store or a docs site though, people just want to get somewhere -- there it's just latency.

    How it's done

    Lenis drives the scroll and feeds its position into GSAP's ticker so ScrollTrigger stays in sync.

    I waited

    until you scrolled here

    to appear.

    ❯ Scroll revealSituational

    Really just a pacing tool -- rewards scrolling, controls the rhythm. Overuse it though, and every section stutters and the whole page feels slow.

    How it's done

    GSAP ScrollTrigger fires a staggered y/opacity tween when each block hits 85% of the viewport.

    Reveal, then depth.

    The window wiped open a second ago -- that's a clip-path reveal. Keep scrolling and the scene inside drifts slower than the word laid over it; your brain reads that speed gap as depth. Two tricks, one moment.

    ❯ Clip-path image revealVanity

    It's just an image that unveils itself instead of sitting there already loaded. Cheap, tasteful, pure decoration -- lovely on a hero, wasted on a spec sheet.

    How it's done

    The window animates from clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0) to a full inset on scroll, wiping left-to-right.

    ❯ ParallaxVanity

    Gorgeous in a demo, but rarely load-bearing. It adds scroll-weight, and it can feel janky on weaker devices. Use it once for impact -- not everywhere.

    How it's done

    Two layers inside the window get scrubbed yPercent tweens with opposite signs, so the scene and the word slide past each other as you scroll.

    →

    Now we're going sideways.

    Keep scrolling down -- the page moves across instead.

    01

    Your scroll wheel still goes down…

    02

    …but the content travels left.

    03

    Vertical input, horizontal output.

    ❯ Horizontal scrollSituational

    Hard to beat for a guided, sequential story. But it hijacks the scroll direction -- disorients people, fights assistive tech. Powerful, and pretty easy to misuse.

    How it's done

    GSAP pins the section and translates the track by (scrollWidth − viewport) as you scroll, scrubbed to your position.

    letters that move

    ❯ Kinetic typeVanity

    Each letter animates in on its own. Delightful for one hero moment, exhausting if the whole site does it. It's a garnish, not a meal.

    How it's done

    The line is split into per-character spans, and the staggered rise is scrubbed to your scroll position -- so it plays while the text is on screen, not before it arrives.

    ENDLESS · MARQUEE · SCROLLING · FOREVER · ENDLESS · MARQUEE · SCROLLING · FOREVER · ENDLESS · MARQUEE · SCROLLING · FOREVER · ENDLESS · MARQUEE · SCROLLING · FOREVER ·
    ❯ MarqueeVanity

    Just an infinite loop of scrolling text. Reads as 'confident and editorial,' but it's decoration -- even its speed follows the dial.

    How it's done

    Two duplicated tracks translate -50% on a CSS keyframe; duration = calc((44 - 34 * var(--dial)) * 1s), so louder = faster.

    Expensive-looking noise.

    This whole page is wearing a film-grain texture -- a faint analog shimmer that reads as "premium" and "considered." Really it's just a looping noise layer doing an impression of 35mm. Toggle it off and watch the page turn clinical.

    ❯ Film grainVanity

    A restless noise laid over everything so a flat digital page reads as filmic and hand-made. Genuinely warms things up -- and it's pure surface. Switch it off and nothing stops working, the page just looks like what it actually is. Basically the definition of a vanity layer: lovely, load-bearing on nothing.

    How it's done

    One fixed <div> with an SVG feTurbulence noise as its background, blended over the page at low opacity. A stepped keyframe jitters it for the flicker; it holds still under prefers-reduced-motion.

    hover me

    Your cursor got bigger, didn't it? A custom cursor just screams "design studio."

    ❯ Custom cursorVanity

    Designers love it -- most visitors don't even notice, and it can hurt accessibility and feel laggy. High risk, low reward. Deploy with care.

    How it's done

    A div follows the pointer via gsap.quickTo, scaling up on interactive elements. Disabled on touch (pointer: coarse).

    That's the Showpiece.

    Loud or quiet -- same engine, different dial.

    ← Back to the vibesGet this for my brand →
    Next vibeThe Product Site →
